perturbation.original_text: As used herein, "Confidential Information" shall be in written, graphic, machine recognizable or other tangible or electronic form and marked "Confidential" or "Proprietary" or shown by implication that it is imparted or disclosed in confidence, or if disclosed orally or visually, shall be reduced to writing in summary form, identified as "Confidential Information" and sent to the Receiving Party within 15 days following such oral or visual disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, "Confidential Information" shall be in written, graphic, machine recognizable or other tangible or electronic form and marked "Confidential" or "Proprietary" or shown by implication that it is imparted or disclosed in confidence, or if disclosed orally or visually, shall be reduced to writing in summary form, identified as "Confidential Information" and sent to the Receiving Party within 365 days following such oral or visual disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notification period for orally disclosed confidential information from 15 days to 365 days creates an unreasonably long delay that is unlikely to be considered commercially reasonable and can violate California's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2204(1) states that a contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement, including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract.  The modified text creates ambiguity as to the terms of disclosure, which might render the contract unenforceable under state law if the courts deem the time to be unreasonable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2204 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract [2201 - 2210] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2204. (1) A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement, including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract. (2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be found even though the moment of its making is undetermined. (3) Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The perturbation introduces a contradiction by changing the notification period for orally disclosed confidential information from 15 days to 365 days, creating a significant delay. The Law Explanation suggests this ambiguity could render the contract unenforceable if the courts deem the time unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Commercial Code § 2204(1), states a contract may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement. The extended 365-day period potentially undermines this agreement by creating uncertainty about the terms of disclosure and potentially conflicting with the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as noted in the Perturbation Explanation, thus creating an inconsistency, as defined by the Perturbation Type definition provided, which results in a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6. All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the disclosing party, and upon the written request of either party, the other party shall promptly return to the disclosing party all Confidential Information disclosed to it and all copies thereof or at the disclosing party's option shall destroy all such Confidential Information and shall provide the receiving party with a certificate that all Confidential Information has been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. All Confidential Information shall remain the property of the disclosing party, and upon the written request of either party, the other party may retain the Confidential Information indefinitely, or at the disclosing party's option shall destroy all such Confidential Information and shall not provide the receiving party with a certificate that all Confidential Information has been destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the agreement to allow the receiving party to retain confidential information indefinitely after a request for return violates the duty of good faith and fair dealing implied in every California contract. Additionally, the destruction of the information without confirmation can lead to legal and financial consequences if the disclosing party is not ensured that the information is properly removed from the receiving party's systems.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1655 states that stipulations which are necessary to make a contract reasonable are implied, in respect to matters concerning which the contract manifests no contrary intention. By removing the 'prompt return' obligation and reversing the burden of responsibility of destruction assurance, the edited text makes the contract unreasonable, and thus unenforceable under state law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1655. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1655. Stipulations which are necessary to make a contract reasonable, or conformable to usage, are implied, in respect to matters concerning which the contract manifests no contrary intention. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code Section 1655. The original text required the receiving party to promptly return confidential information upon request. The changed text allows the receiving party to retain the confidential information indefinitely. This violates the implied stipulation to make the contract reasonable, as stated in the law snippet: 'Stipulations which are necessary to make a contract reasonable...are implied, in respect to matters concerning which the contract manifests no contrary intention.' By removing the obligation to return confidential information, the edited text makes the contract unreasonable and thus unenforceable under state law, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9. The obligations undertaken by each party pursuant to this Agreement shall remain in effect for three years from the last date of disclosure of Confidential Information, and shall survive any termination or expiration hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. The obligations undertaken by each party pursuant to this Agreement shall remain in effect for ninety days from the last date of disclosure of Confidential Information, and shall survive any termination or expiration hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period from three years to ninety days (approximately 3 months) is an unreasonably short period for trade secrets in California. This could be deemed insufficient under California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), Cal. Civ. Code § 3426 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: While CUTSA does not specify a minimum duration for confidentiality, reducing it to 90 days may be deemed inadequate to protect trade secrets, especially considering the time and resources invested in developing them. This modification could undermine the protections afforded by CUTSA, rendering the confidentiality agreement ineffective for its intended purpose.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The change from three years to ninety days for confidentiality potentially contradicts the purpose of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as it may be deemed inadequate to protect trade secrets. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest the reduced timeframe is insufficient to safeguard trade secrets, especially considering the investment in their development. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the relevant law (California Uniform Trade Secrets Act), suggesting the original three-year period is more defensible than 90 days under the legal framework, because the reduced timeframe undermines the protections afforded by CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: 12. This Agreement may be modified only by written amendment signed by both parties. This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California without regard to the conflict of laws provisions and shall be subject to the jurisdiction of the courts of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. This Agreement may be modified orally. This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of Haiti and shall be subject to the jurisdiction of the courts of Haiti.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Haiti and allowing for oral modifications contradicts the California statute of frauds, which requires certain contracts, including those not to be performed within a year, to be in writing. This also changes the jurisdiction to a foreign one, making it harder to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1624 outlines contracts that must be in writing, and modifies the provisions of how the contract may be enforced in the legal system. It also introduces enforceability and jurisdictional challenges due to the contract being interpreted and litigated under Haitian law, which may differ significantly from California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1624. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a longer period
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Civil Code § 1624(a)(1), as provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which requires that agreements not to be performed within a year from the making thereof must be in writing. The changed text allows for oral modifications, which would violate this requirement. The original text required written amendments signed by both parties, aligning with the statute of frauds. Additionally, changing the governing law and jurisdiction to Haiti undermines the enforceability of the contract under California law, further contradicting the original agreement's adherence to California legal standards.
